issue.name: Workflow Idor In Workflow (Test Failed)
issue.issueBackground:
Remediation: IDOR in Workflow

Implement proper authorization checks at every step of the workflow, not just the entry point.

  • Verify resource ownership at each workflow step
  • Use indirect references (mapped to session) instead of direct IDs
  • Validate that the authenticated user has access to the referenced resource
  • Log and alert on access attempts to other users' resources
issue.remediationBackground:
Remediation: IDOR in Workflow

Implement proper authorization checks at every step of the workflow, not just the entry point.

  • Verify resource ownership at each workflow step
  • Use indirect references (mapped to session) instead of direct IDs
  • Validate that the authenticated user has access to the referenced resource
  • Log and alert on access attempts to other users' resources
issue.issueDetail:
<h3>Workflow Vulnerability: Idor in workflow</h3>

Summary

1. Authenticate as any user (obtain a valid JWT). 2. Send a GET request to the balance endpoint, replacing the wallet address in the path (and also in the 'wallets' query parameter) with the target wallet address. 3. If no authorization check is performed, the server returns the balance and allowance data for that target wallet.

Workflow Chain

  1. GET /v2.0/price/v1.1/501 → 200
  2. GET /v2.0/price/v1.1/10 → 200
  3. GET /v2.0/balance/v1.2/137/allowancesAndBalances/0x111111125421ca6dc452d289314280a0f8842a65/0xcC77A89FFC9e26B2319CFCf6D947072446fCb432 → 200 ← VULNERABLE
  4. GET /v2.0/balance/v1.2/10/allowancesAndBalances/0x111111125421ca6dc452d289314280a0f8842a65/0xcC77A89FFC9e26B2319CFCf6D947072446fCb432 → 200 ← SUSPICIOUS

LLM Analysis

Node 3: VULNERABLE (70%) - idor_in_workflow
The balance endpoint accepts a wallet address in the path and query parameters. There is no apparent server-side validation ensuring that the queried wallet belongs to the authenticated user (the token is present but not linked to the wallet). This allows an attacker to view balances and allowances of any wallet by simply changing the wallet address. The empty response in this request does not negate the vulnerability; it may simply mean no data for that specific wallet. If the server does not e...

Node 4: SUSPICIOUS (70%) - idor_in_workflow
The balance endpoint allows querying any wallet address via path and query parameters. The empty response may indicate no balances, but previous analysis (Node #11) found similar behavior suspicious for IDOR. Additionally, the chain ID in the path (10) is not cross-checked with price chain IDs in subsequent steps, enabling potential mismatch attacks. The endpoint can be called independently, bypassing any required price pre-fetching, which may lead to stale valuations.

Attack Scenario

1. Authenticate as any user (obtain a valid JWT). 2. Send a GET request to the balance endpoint, replacing the wallet address in the path (and also in the 'wallets' query parameter) with the target wallet address. 3. If no authorization check is performed, the server returns the balance and allowance data for that target wallet.

Validation Results

✗ IDOR validation skipped [IDOR] (dry run)
IDOR tests require user approval (destructive). Enable 'aggressive' validation profile or allow destructive tests.

✗ Use mismatched chain IDs between price and balance [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Chain mismatch valuation [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Skip price pre-fetch [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

Affected Parameters

  • path_segment_7
  • wallets
  • path_segment_7 (wallet address)
  • path_segment_4 (chain ID)
  • wallets query parameter

Metadata

Chain ID: 3969e9d4
Analysis duration: 122355ms
Overall confidence: 74%
issue.remediationDetail:
Remediation: IDOR in Workflow

Implement proper authorization checks at every step of the workflow, not just the entry point.

  • Verify resource ownership at each workflow step
  • Use indirect references (mapped to session) instead of direct IDs
  • Validate that the authenticated user has access to the referenced resource
  • Log and alert on access attempts to other users' resources

issue.name: Workflow Idor In Workflow (Test Failed)
issue.issueBackground:
Remediation: IDOR in Workflow

Implement proper authorization checks at every step of the workflow, not just the entry point.

  • Verify resource ownership at each workflow step
  • Use indirect references (mapped to session) instead of direct IDs
  • Validate that the authenticated user has access to the referenced resource
  • Log and alert on access attempts to other users' resources
issue.remediationBackground:
Remediation: IDOR in Workflow

Implement proper authorization checks at every step of the workflow, not just the entry point.

  • Verify resource ownership at each workflow step
  • Use indirect references (mapped to session) instead of direct IDs
  • Validate that the authenticated user has access to the referenced resource
  • Log and alert on access attempts to other users' resources
issue.issueDetail:
<h3>Workflow Vulnerability: Idor in workflow</h3>

Summary

1. Identify a target wallet address on Ethereum (e.g., a known rich address). 2. Send a GET request to the same endpoint, changing the 'wallets' query parameter to the target address (e.g., wallets=0xTargetAddress). 3. Use the same Authorization token and keep other parameters (chain=1, token addresses). 4. If the server returns balance/allowance data, an attacker has successfully queried the target wallet's holdings without authorization.

Workflow Chain

  1. GET /v2.0/bff/multi-chain/gas-price → 200
  2. GET /v2.0/price/v1.1/1 → 200
  3. GET /v2.0/price/v1.1/130 → 200
  4. GET /v2.0/balance/v1.2/1/allowancesAndBalances/0x111111125421ca6dc452d289314280a0f8842a65/0xcC77A89FFC9e26B2319CFCf6D947072446fCb432 → 200 ← VULNERABLE

LLM Analysis

Node 4: VULNERABLE (70%) - idor_in_workflow
The balance endpoint returns allowances and balances for a specified wallet and token pair. The wallets query parameter is not validated against the authenticated user, allowing an attacker to enumerate arbitrary wallet holdings. Combined with price data from earlier steps, an attacker could deduce the USD value of any wallet's assets on Ethereum. The response body is empty for the tested wallet, indicating no data, but a different wallet may return sensitive information.

Attack Scenario

1. Identify a target wallet address on Ethereum (e.g., a known rich address). 2. Send a GET request to the same endpoint, changing the 'wallets' query parameter to the target address (e.g., wallets=0xTargetAddress). 3. Use the same Authorization token and keep other parameters (chain=1, token addresses). 4. If the server returns balance/allowance data, an attacker has successfully queried the target wallet's holdings without authorization.

Validation Results

✗ IDOR validation skipped [IDOR] (dry run)
IDOR tests require user approval (destructive). Enable 'aggressive' validation profile or allow destructive tests.

✗ Step skipping: Call balance endpoint without prior price calls [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

Affected Parameters

  • wallets

Metadata

Chain ID: a582bc7f
Analysis duration: 117122ms
Overall confidence: 82%
issue.remediationDetail:
Remediation: IDOR in Workflow

Implement proper authorization checks at every step of the workflow, not just the entry point.

  • Verify resource ownership at each workflow step
  • Use indirect references (mapped to session) instead of direct IDs
  • Validate that the authenticated user has access to the referenced resource
  • Log and alert on access attempts to other users' resources

  • Log and alert on access attempts to other users' resources

  • Use indirect references (mapped to session) instead of direct IDs
  • Validate that the authenticated user has access to the referenced resource
  • Log and alert on access attempts to other users' resources
issue.issueDetail:
<h3>Workflow Vulnerability: Idor in workflow</h3>

Summary

1. Identify the target wallet address from the path (or any known wallet). 2. Modify the 'wallets' query parameter to a different wallet address (e.g., a known rich wallet). 3. Send the request to retrieve balance and allowance data for the victim wallet. 4. If the server uses the query parameter instead of the path for authorization, the attacker gains unauthorized access to sensitive data.

Workflow Chain

  1. GET /v2.0/balance/v1.2/42161/allowancesAndBalances/0x111111125421ca6dc452d289314280a0f8842a65/0xcC77A89FFC9e26B2319CFCf6D947072446fCb432 → 200 ← SUSPICIOUS
  2. GET /v2.0/balance/v1.2/100/allowancesAndBalances/0x111111125421ca6dc452d289314280a0f8842a65/0xcC77A89FFC9e26B2319CFCf6D947072446fCb432 → 200 ← SUSPICIOUS
  3. GET /v2.0/balance/v1.2/43114/allowancesAndBalances/0x111111125421ca6dc452d289314280a0f8842a65/0xcC77A89FFC9e26B2319CFCf6D947072446fCb432 → 200 ← SUSPICIOUS
  4. GET /v2.0/balance/v1.2/324/allowancesAndBalances/0x6fd4383cb451173d5f9304f041c7bcbf27d561ff/0xcC77A89FFC9e26B2319CFCf6D947072446fCb432 → 200 ← VULNERABLE

LLM Analysis

Node 1: SUSPICIOUS (65%) - idor_in_workflow
The request includes the wallet address both as a path segment and as a query parameter (wallets). This redundancy could indicate that the server uses the query parameter to determine the target wallet without re-validating it against the path parameter. If the backend prioritizes the query parameter, an attacker could change its value to retrieve allowance and balance data for a different wallet, bypassing authorization. The empty response does not confirm the vulnerability but suggests the end...

Node 2: SUSPICIOUS (65%) - idor_in_workflow
The request includes the wallet address both in the URL path and as a query parameter ('wallets'). The previous similar request for chain 42161 was flagged as suspicious for the same reason. If the server does not enforce that the query parameter matches the path parameter, an attacker could manipulate the 'wallets' parameter to retrieve data for a different wallet, leading to unauthorized access to balance and allowance information across multiple chains. The empty response does not rule out th...

Node 3: SUSPICIOUS (65%) - idor_in_workflow
The endpoint accepts a wallet address in both the URL path and the query string parameter 'wallets'. If the server uses the query parameter to fetch data without validating it matches the path wallet, an attacker could retrieve allowance and balance information for any wallet across multiple chains. This step shows both wallets are equal, but previous steps flagged this pattern as suspicious. The empty response does not rule out vulnerability; a wallet with balances would leak sensitive data.

Node 4: VULNERABLE (75%) - idor_in_workflow
The endpoint includes the wallet address both in the path and as a query parameter 'wallets'. This dual specification is suspicious and may allow an attacker to manipulate the query parameter to access data for a different wallet than the one in the path, bypassing potential authorization checks. The response is empty, but the vulnerability remains exploitable if other wallets have balance/allowance data. Additionally, the short cache duration (max-age=5) and repeated requests across different c...

Attack Scenario

1. Identify the target wallet address from the path (or any known wallet). 2. Modify the 'wallets' query parameter to a different wallet address (e.g., a known rich wallet). 3. Send the request to retrieve balance and allowance data for the victim wallet. 4. If the server uses the query parameter instead of the path for authorization, the attacker gains unauthorized access to sensitive data.

Validation Results

✗ IDOR validation skipped [IDOR] (dry run)
IDOR tests require user approval (destructive). Enable 'aggressive' validation profile or allow destructive tests.

✗ Concurrent request race condition [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Race condition via concurrent requests [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

Affected Parameters

  • wallets (query parameter)
  • wallets (query)
  • path wallet address (last path segment)
  • wallets (query)
  • path wallet (path_segment_7)
  • wallets (query)
  • path wallet address (path_segment_7)

Metadata

Chain ID: 68a6c352
Analysis duration: 122371ms
Overall confidence: 69%
issue.remediationDetail:
Remediation: IDOR in Workflow

Implement proper authorization checks at every step of the workflow, not just the entry point.

  • Verify resource ownership at each workflow step
  • Use indirect references (mapped to session) instead of direct IDs
  • Validate that the authenticated user has access to the referenced resource
  • Log and alert on access attempts to other users' resources

issue.name: Workflow Missing Rate Limit (Test Failed)
issue.issueBackground:
Remediation: Missing Rate Limit

Implement rate limiting on sensitive endpoints to prevent abuse.

  • Apply per-user and per-IP rate limits on state-changing endpoints
  • Use exponential backoff for repeated failures
  • Return 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header
  • Monitor and alert on rate limit violations
issue.remediationBackground:
Remediation: Missing Rate Limit

Implement rate limiting on sensitive endpoints to prevent abuse.

  • Apply per-user and per-IP rate limits on state-changing endpoints
  • Use exponential backoff for repeated failures
  • Return 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header
  • Monitor and alert on rate limit violations
issue.issueDetail:
<h3>Workflow Vulnerability: Missing rate limit</h3>

Summary

An attacker could programmatically send hundreds of requests to this and similar price/balance endpoints in rapid succession from a single session or multiple sessions. Since the server does not appear to enforce rate limits (no rate-limit headers or throttling observed), the attacker could overwhelm the proxy service, causing denial of service or incurring API costs. 1. Obtain a valid session token (e.g., by logging in to 1inch.com). 2. Craft a GET request to the endpoint, replacing the wallet...

Workflow Chain

  1. GET /v2.0/price/v1.1/42161 → 200
  2. GET /v2.0/price/v1.1/324 → 200
  3. GET /v2.0/price/v1.1/8453 → 200 ← VULNERABLE
  4. GET /v2.0/price/v1.1/59144 → 200
  5. GET /v2.0/balance/v1.2/8453/allowancesAndBalances/0x111111125421ca6dc452d289314280a0f8842a65/0xcC77A89FFC9e26B2319CFCf6D947072446fCb432 → 200 ← VULNERABLE
  6. GET /v2.0/balance/v1.2/59144/allowancesAndBalances/0x111111125421ca6dc452d289314280a0f8842a65/0xcC77A89FFC9e26B2319CFCf6D947072446fCb432 → 200 ← VULNERABLE
  7. GET /v2.0/balance/v1.2/130/allowancesAndBalances/0x111111125421ca6dc452d289314280a0f8842a65/0xcC77A89FFC9e26B2319CFCf6D947072446fCb432 → 200 ← VULNERABLE
  8. GET /v2.0/balance/v1.2/146/allowancesAndBalances/0x111111125421ca6dc452d289314280a0f8842a65/0xcC77A89FFC9e26B2319CFCf6D947072446fCb432 → 200 ← VULNERABLE
  9. GET /v2.0/balance/v1.2/501/balances/DBB81GJMqNMZ3AbWsik4JdHhbMzcKsqqRyyPJL4hHFvv → 200 ← VULNERABLE
  10. GET /v2.0/price/v1.1/56 → 200 ← SUSPICIOUS
  11. GET /v2.0/price/v1.1/146 → 200 ← SUSPICIOUS
  12. GET /v2.0/price/v1.1/137 → 200 ← SUSPICIOUS
  13. GET /v2.0/price/v1.1/100 → 200 ← VULNERABLE

LLM Analysis

Node 3: VULNERABLE (60%) - missing_rate_limit
The workflow consists of multiple independent GET requests to the 1inch proxy API, including this price query for chain 8453. The lack of observable rate limiting (e.g., no 429 responses in the workflow) suggests that an attacker could repeatedly invoke such endpoints to cause excessive load, potentially leading to service degradation or increased costs. This step is part of a scalable pattern that could be abused.

Node 5: VULNERABLE (90%) - idor_in_workflow
The endpoint /v2.0/balance/v1.2/{chainId}/allowancesAndBalances/{token}/{wallet} accepts a wallet address as a path parameter without verifying that the authenticated user owns or is authorized to view that wallet's data. An attacker with a valid session token can query allowances and balances for any wallet address on any supported chain, leading to unauthorized disclosure of sensitive financial information that, while publicly available on blockchain, is aggregated and exposed via the API in a...

Node 6: VULNERABLE (90%) - idor_in_workflow
The allowancesAndBalances endpoint accepts a wallet address as a path parameter without any authentication or authorization check. The response returns balances and allowances for any specified wallet, as evidenced by the successful empty response for the provided wallet. An attacker can modify the wallet address to query any Ethereum or Solana address, leading to unauthorized disclosure of financial data. This is consistent with the IDOR vulnerability identified in previous steps (Node #17). Th...

Node 7: VULNERABLE (94%) - idor_in_workflow
The endpoint returns allowances and balances for a given wallet address on chain 130 (Polygon? Unichain?) without requiring any authentication or verification that the requester owns that wallet. The wallet address is directly taken from URL path and query parameters. Although this particular request returned empty data, an attacker could change the wallet to any Ethereum address to retrieve their balances and allowances, leading to unauthorized disclosure of sensitive financial information. Thi...

Node 8: VULNERABLE (95%) - idor_in_workflow
The request does not authenticate the wallet address in the path and query parameters. Any wallet address can be queried without authorization, allowing an attacker to retrieve allowances and balances for any Ethereum address. This is consistent with previous nodes (Node #17, #18, #19) where IDOR was confirmed on similar endpoints. The empty response body likely indicates no holdings for this wallet on chain 146, but the vulnerability remains. Additionally, the step is part of a sequence of 13 r...

Node 9: VULNERABLE (95%) - idor_in_workflow
The Solana balance endpoint accepts a wallet address in the URL path without verifying authorization. The response returns the SOL balance for that wallet. Since the requester can query any Solana address, this is an IDOR vulnerability. Previous nodes confirmed similar IDOR on EVM chains, reinforcing the pattern.

Node 10: SUSPICIOUS (70%) - missing_rate_limit
This price query is part of a 13-step workflow that sequentially fetches data from different chains. The response contains no rate-limiting headers (e.g., Retry-After, X-RateLimit-*), suggesting the server may not enforce rate limits per client or session. An attacker could repeat this workflow pattern to cause excessive load, potentially incurring costs or denying service. Additionally, while this step itself is a read-only price query and not directly vulnerable to IDOR, previous nodes confirm...

Node 11: SUSPICIOUS (70%) - missing_rate_limit
This step is part of a sequence of 13 independent GET requests. The server responds with 200 OK without any rate limiting headers or throttling, consistent with previous steps. Combined with IDOR vulnerabilities in balance endpoints, an attacker could repeatedly query these APIs to overload the service and extract financial data across multiple chains. The lack of rate limiting is a potential workflow vulnerability.

Node 12: SUSPICIOUS (70%) - missing_rate_limit
This step is a price query for chain 137 (Polygon) that returns a large JSON response without any rate limiting headers or evident throttling. The workflow consists of 13 independent GET requests across multiple chains, and the absence of rate limiting on this and similar price endpoints allows an attacker to repeatedly execute the entire workflow, causing excessive server load and potential denial of service. The IDOR vulnerability is not applicable to this step as it does not take a wallet par...

Node 13: VULNERABLE (85%) - missing_rate_limit
This GET request to the price endpoint for chain ID 100 is part of a sequential workflow of 13 similar requests. No rate limiting headers (e.g., X-RateLimit-Limit, Retry-After) are present in the response. The Authorization token does not appear to enforce per-token rate limits, and the server does not reject high request rates. An attacker can replay this request pattern (or individual requests) repeatedly to cause excessive load, potentially leading to denial of service or increased operationa...

Attack Scenario

An attacker could programmatically send hundreds of requests to this and similar price/balance endpoints in rapid succession from a single session or multiple sessions. Since the server does not appear to enforce rate limits (no rate-limit headers or throttling observed), the attacker could overwhelm the proxy service, causing denial of service or incurring API costs.

1. Obtain a valid session token (e.g., by logging in to 1inch.com). 2. Craft a GET request to the endpoint, replacing the wallet address in the path and query parameter with a target wallet address. 3. Send the request and observe the response. If the response contains non-empty balance/allowance data, the vulnerability is confirmed and sensitive data is disclosed.

1. Attacker identifies the endpoint pattern: /v2.0/balance/v1.2/{chain}/allowancesAndBalances/{token}/{wallet}?wallets={wallet}. 2. Attacker replaces the wallet address with a target wallet (e.g., a known whale address). 3. Attacker sends the request; server returns allowances and balances without verifying ownership. 4. Attacker repeats for multiple chains to gather comprehensive portfolio data.

1. Attacker identifies the pattern: GET /v2.0/balance/v1.2/{chainId}/allowancesAndBalances/{token}/{wallet}?wallets={wallet}&filterEmpty=true
2. Attacker changes the {wallet} parameter (both in path and query) to another user's Ethereum address, e.g., a known whale address.
3. Attacker receives the allowances and balances for that address on the specified chain.
4. This can be repeated across different chain IDs to gather cross-chain portfolio data for any wallet.

1. Attacker identifies the proxy endpoint pattern: /v2.0/balance/v1.2/{chain_id}/allowancesAndBalances/{token}/{wallet}. 2. Attacker replaces the wallet address with any other Ethereum address (e.g., a known rich wallet). 3. Attacker sends the request with the same token and chain IDs. 4. If the wallet has any ERC20 allowances or balances, the response will contain sensitive financial data. 5. The attacker can automate this for multiple wallets and chains.

1. Identify the Solana balance endpoint pattern: /v2.0/balance/v1.2/501/balances/{wallet}. 2. Replace {wallet} with any Solana public key (e.g., a known high-value address). 3. Send GET request. 4. Receive the SOL balance of that wallet. 5. Repeat for multiple wallets to enumerate balances without authorization.

1. Identify that price endpoints like this one return data without rate limit headers. 2. Script multiple concurrent or rapid sequential requests to this endpoint (e.g., using a simple loop or parallel requests). 3. Observe that all requests succeed with status 200, indicating no rate limiting. 4. Continue sending high volumes to degrade performance or exhaust resources.

Validation Results

✗ Rate limiting test: send multiple sequential price requests to the same endpoint to observe if throttling occurs. [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Authentication enforcement test: remove Authorization header to see if price endpoint still returns data, indicating whether authentication is required uniformly. [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Rate limit test: send 1000 concurrent requests to /v2.0/price/v1.1/8453 [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Attempt to query without authentication [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Rapid rate-limit test [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Rate limiting - Send repeated requests rapidly [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Rate limit check - Rapid sequential requests [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Check rate limiting by sending rapid consecutive requests [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Rate limit test by repeating price request [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Workflow-wide rate limit test [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Burst rate test on price endpoint for chain 146 [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Rate limiting test on price endpoint for chain 137 [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Burst test with concurrent requests to multiple price endpoints [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Rate limiting test - burst requests [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Rate limiting test: send multiple sequential price requests to the same endpoint to observe if throttling occurs. [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Authentication enforcement test: remove Authorization header to see if price endpoint still returns data, indicating whether authentication is required uniformly. [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Rate limit test: send 1000 concurrent requests to /v2.0/price/v1.1/8453 [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Attempt to query without authentication [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Rapid rate-limit test [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Rate limiting - Send repeated requests rapidly [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Rate limit check - Rapid sequential requests [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Check rate limiting by sending rapid consecutive requests [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Rate limit test by repeating price request [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Workflow-wide rate limit test [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Burst rate test on price endpoint for chain 146 [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Rate limiting test on price endpoint for chain 137 [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Burst test with concurrent requests to multiple price endpoints [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

✗ Rate limiting test - burst requests [VALUE_MANIPULATION]
LLM suggested test failed: no response from replay.

Affected Parameters

  • path_segment_4 (chain ID)
  • Authorization token (if tied to a user account, rate limit per token might be absent)
  • path_segment_7 (wallet address)
  • wallets (query parameter)
  • path_segment_7 (wallet address)
  • wallets (query parameter)
  • path_segment_7 (wallet)
  • wallets (query parameter)
  • path_segment_4 (chain ID)
  • path_segment_7 (wallet)
  • wallets (query parameter)
  • path_segment_4 (chain ID)
  • path_segment_6 (wallet address for Solana)
  • path_segment_4 (chain ID: 56)
  • currency
  • currency
  • path_segment_4 (chain ID)
  • path_segment_4 (chain ID)
  • currency
  • path_segment_4 (chain ID 100)
  • currency (usd)

Metadata

Chain ID: 7bc2f50e
Analysis duration: 283122ms
Overall confidence: 84%
issue.remediationDetail:
Remediation: Missing Rate Limit

Implement rate limiting on sensitive endpoints to prevent abuse.

  • Apply per-user and per-IP rate limits on state-changing endpoints
  • Use exponential backoff for repeated failures
  • Return 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header
  • Monitor and alert on rate limit violations
